steps.title: Identify the grammatical structures and transition word
steps.content:
First clause: “One might assume cases like this... to be rare” = independent clause.
Second clause: “they are counted among a litany of examples” = independent clause.
The phrase “on the contrary” is a conjunctive adverb that signals a contrast between these two complete thoughts.

steps.title: Apply the conjunctive adverb punctuation rule
steps.content:
When two independent clauses are joined by a conjunctive adverb, the standard pattern is semicolon before, comma after: IC; conjunctive adverb, IC.

Choice D applies this rule perfectly: “to be rare; on the contrary, they are counted among a litany of examples.” The other options either fail to separate the independent clauses or violate the standard punctuation pattern for conjunctive adverbs.

That matches choice D — D is correct.
